highlight.title: Major BGP Outages Demonstrate Internet's Fragile Foundation
highlight.summary: A series of BGP routing incidents in early 2026, including major Cloudflare outages and route leaks affecting services like Discord and AWS, have exposed how easily misconfigurations can take down large portions of the internet. These failures show that despite decades of development, the internet's routing system remains vulnerable to both human error and potential attacks.

resources.title: How the Internet Works: A Complete Guide for 2026
resources.why_now: Perfect timing as major BGP outages have teenagers asking how their internet actually works. This guide explains everything from DNS to TCP/IP in accessible terms, covering both fundamentals and 2026's emerging technologies like 6G and AI-driven networks.

resources.title: What Happens When You Type a URL in the Browser
resources.why_now: With recent internet outages making headlines, this technical deep-dive into DNS resolution, TCP handshakes, and HTTP requests helps professionals understand exactly where failures can occur. Essential reading after witnessing how BGP misconfigurations brought down major services.

resources.title: BGP Vortex: Internet Routing Vortices Create Outages by Preventing Convergence
resources.why_now: Researchers have discovered a new vulnerability that could impact 96% of internet networks by exploiting BGP routing policies. This isn't theoretical—lab tests show single attacks can cause 37-second outages, making this essential reading for understanding internet stability risks.

resources.title: Internet for Beginners: How Devices Connect and How the Internet Works
resources.why_now: After major routing failures grabbed headlines, many are realizing they don't understand how their Wi-Fi actually connects to the global internet. This guide walks through everything from Ethernet cables to routers to data centers in simple terms that anyone can follow.

claude_take: The recent wave of BGP outages isn't just technical news—it's a wake-up call about how fragile our digital world really is. While we've built an internet that feels magical and instant, it's actually held together by protocols from the 1980s that can be broken by a single misconfigured router, affecting millions instantly.
